[IMAGE:0]It's a little before midnight, so we can proceed with a new Magic Monday. Ask me anything about occultism and I'll do my best to answer it. With certain exceptions, any question received by midnight Monday Eastern time will get an answer. Please note:  Any question or comment received after then will not get an answer, and in fact will just be deleted. (I've been getting an increasing number of people trying to post after these are closed, so will have to draw a harder line than before.) If you're in a hurry, or suspect you may be the 143,916th person to ask a question, please check out the very rough version 1.1 of The Magic Monday FAQ here. Also: I will not be putting through or answering any more questions about practicing magic around children. I've answered those in simple declarative sentences in the FAQ. If you read the FAQ and don't think your question has been answered, read it again. If that doesn't help, consider remedial reading classes; yes, it really is as simple and straightforward as the FAQ says. 

The image? I've had several people ask me now whether there will be a Magic Monday this week, since the Monday in question is Christmas. The answer, of course, is yes. My wife and I celebrated the solstice in proper form last Thursday -- one of the benefits of being a Druid is that you get to open your holiday presents four days early -- and today is just another Monday to me.  I certainly wish all my Christian readers a happy Christmas, and those of my other readers who do the Santa Claus thing, why, have a fine time at it -- but that's not a universal habit by any means, especially not these days. No doubt it'll be a quieter Magic Monday than usual, but I'm good with that -- I've got three books by other authors to review and write blurbs for before the first of the year.

Oh, and the bear?  In the branch of the Druid tradition I practice, the bear's the symbolic animal of the winter solstice. The hawk fills that role for the spring equinox, the legendary white stag for the summer solstice, and the salmon of wisdom for the autumn equinox. So here's a fine solstice bear sniffing the winter wind, bringing my blessings for the season to all who will accept them.

You can use either of the links above to access my online tip jar; Buymeacoffee is good for small tips, Ko-Fi is better for larger ones. (I used to use PayPal but they developed an allergy to free speech, so I've developed an allergy to them.) If you're interested in political and economic astrology, or simply prefer to use a subscription service to support your favorite authors, you can find my Patreon page here and my SubscribeStar page here.

Bookshop.org is an alternative online bookstore that supports local bookstores and authors, which a certain gargantuan corporation doesn't, and I have a shop there, which you can check out here. Please consider patronizing it if you'd like to purchase any of my books online.

And don't forget to look up your Pangalactic New Age Soul Signature at CosmicOom.com.

With that said, have at it!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
That's a complicated matter, and of course it's more complicated for me because I didn't grow up in a Christian family -- I was never baptized, for example. When I was a kid very few people in the neighborhood where I lived went to church, and the rest of us thought they were kind of weird. Later, of course, Protestant evangelical fundamentalism was all the rage, and so my first real exposure to Christianity was via one of its most intolerant and arrogant forms. It was quite a bit later that I had the opportunity to get a less jaundiced view of the faith.

When you comment that Christianity's unoriginal, of course, you're quite correct, but that's true of pretty much all religions. Buddhism took a lot from Hindu sources, Judaism drew heavily from Canaanite religion in its early days and Zoroastrianism later on, Christianity borrowed from most of the religious traditions of late classical antiquity -- nothing new in that. The habit of demonizing the sources from which they borrowed so much is gauche, to use no harsher word, but it's hard to avoid once you've committed yourself to the claim that your faith is the only true one.

The crucial point to realize, though, is that Christianity is a family of religions rather than a single faith, and the different branches have very different magical implications. The sacramental rituals of Catholic and Orthodox churches embody one set of magic, the intense focus on scriptural study and prayer of the more traditional Protestant churches embody a very different set, and then there are the wishy-washy mainstream churches, which are losing members at a rapid pace because they've basically given up having any magic at all.

I'm guessing from one of your comments, though, that you're mostly asking about Protestant Christianity of the Baptist sort. From my perspective, its magic sets up an ideal image of what human beings ought to be like and encourages its believers to try to emulate that image. Since they inevitably fail, and are set up to fail -- "all have sinned and fallen short of the glory of God" -- that generates strong feelings of guilt and shame, which provide emotional energy to the egregor of the church, and it also encourages them to pour out their admiration on Jesus, the one who didn't fail -- another potent source of energy. Both those feed the egregor or group mind of the church, and are gathered up and directed in church services.

Back in the days when ministers knew how to work with that energy, the result was a certain number of miracles and other benefits. Nowadays, since that knowledge has mostly been forgotten, the energy pools in the ministers, gets stagnant, and vents via the usual outlet between the legs -- thus the unending drumbeat of sex scandals in such churches. Embarrassing as those are, the ministers aren't any more depraved than the rest of us -- they're just overwhelmed with energies they have never been taught to handle.

Regarding the respective solstice and Christmas celebrations themselves, I've never had much difficulty reconciling and accepting both. I think whichever Emperor or Pope set the current date of Christmas knew what he was doing, and there was much more to it than co-opting Sol Invictus's festival or Saturnalia. If one keeps in mind that Winter Is Serious Business, it's easier to see the hope and promise represented by a newborn Savior and the hope represented by lengthening days from the very beginning of the coldest part of winter as different perspectives on the same deeper idea.

The lyrics of some of the older Christmas hymns (aka Christmas carols) in the lesser-known latter verses are consistent with this view. The frequent mentions of cold, dark, and snow aren't props for pretty scenery for greeting cards; they refer to existential fear that the light of stars, candles, hearths and ultimately salvation are set in contrast to.

Blessings of the season everyone!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
I've known people who used the Enochian material intensively and got very good results from it. I've also known people who used it, and crashed and burned. I don't think Case was right to blame Crowley's fate on his use of Enochian -- Crowley had plenty of other reasons for messing up the way he did -- but the Enochian system is powerful, dangerous, and emphatically not for everybody.

Case was wrong that the tablets were a rigmarole, and he was wrong, I think, to take the stories circulated about Kelly and Dee too literally -- I don't think Case had studied the Steganographia of Trithemius, so he didn't know just how many-layered something like Dee's diaries might be (or how far from literal truth they might be, depending on what they were meant to conceal). Of course his own project also came to a bit of an ignominious end -- he was one of the last of the American occultists who believed that physical immortality was within reach, and of course that didn't work too well for him. But for most students of occultism, Case's advice is not necessarily mistaken. It's got its advantages, but you can do anything you need to do in magic without it, using approaches that are much less likely to blow up in your face.

comments.title: Merry Christmas, Modified Five Rites, Ancient City
comments.content:
Good Evening,

Merry Christmas to all who mark it! And whatever Winter Solstice-linked holiday you may or may not celebrate, may the rekindling of light bring you hope in this dark time.

To Share: 1. I've been experimenting with some of the tweaks to the Five Rites that came to me after reading The Secret of the Five Rites, namely, a. I've incorporated the steps of the Judson exercise, b. I'm trying to make the second rite more like a "proper" camel pose, and c. I've been doing the Fifth Rite as a "proper" Dand/"Hindu Push Up." Here's what I can report:
a. I begin the Five Rites by facing north and then spinning counter-clockwise a certain number of times, ending up facing south, as in the Judson Exercise. I'm not /sure/ how many to do, I've been experimenting with that, but around half of the number of repetitions I'm doing of each Rite seems about right (for example, I'm currently doing 11 repetitions of each Rite, so I did 5.5 counter-clockwise spins). After this, I follow the Judson Exercise steps to stand on tip-toes with heels together and hands out, fingers splayed to absorb life force from the earth, then raise my hands, look up, and slowly rotate 1/2 turn to face north again, calling down life from the Sky/Sun. After that, I do all Five Rites, with a short pause for three long breaths between each. After I finish the last Rite, I stand for a time with legs hip-to-shoulder width apart, and my hands forming a diamond on my belly, thumbs up and on my navel. I breath in through my nose and slowly out through pursed lips, trying to put my focus on my belly and torso (I believe I got this form of "grounding" from brendhelm, and I think it's from Chinese martial arts). Lastly, I turn south and do three "sweeps" of each hand to seal my etheric body. Doing this for the past week, I have nothing much different from the standard Five Rites to report, but I feel good, energetic, and with a clear head afterward.
b. I have found tips on what to pay attention to in your body and how to go into and out of a camel pose helpful for this, but I have also found that it's hard to get into as deep a stretch as a fully-executed camel pose when following the Five Rites approach of multiple repetitions (i.e. bend back, come back up, repeat). That may just be that I'm not that flexible. Top tips that have been helping me: focus on "pulling" up through your thighs and lower belly and on "straightening" or pulling "down" with the tail bone to stabilize the lower body during this, keep your thumbs on your sacrum while lowering and raising, and imagine your heart pulling up and over in an arc, rather than straight backwards. If you do video, I found this tutorial from Yoga by Adrienne helpful: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=AigVwIFvmAQ
c. Turning the Fifth Rite into a real dand has resulted in a much more robust upper body workout than I was getting from it previously. Specifically, the "diving" motion going from downward dog into upward dog engages the pecs and triceps a lot more (as you bend your arms, you tuck your elbows into your sides and focus on moving your chin, chest, belly as close to the floor/mat as you can get them while coming up into the end pose). This is enough more challenging that even if you have worked up to all 21 repetitions with the Rites, you may want to back down to a lower number until it's comfortable. Again, if you do video, I found this guy helpful: https://www.moderncalisthenics.com/how-to-hindu-push-up/

2. I've looked back on my past year and forward to the coming one here: https://jpowellrussell.com/#looking_back_on_2023_and_forward_to_2024

As always, thanks very much to JMG and everyone else here for all that you do.

My blessings to all who welcome them,

comments.content:
Greetings All,

I’m sorry if you’ve answered this before John. I’m currently noticing a fairly regular attack on magical practitioners for using the Hermetic Qabalah. This is usually based on the claim that we are appropriating Jewish culture. Can you please confirm the origins of the Jewish Kabbalah? The great Jewish musician/ magician Z’ev claimed it was taught to the Jews in Babylon and was personally keen on disseminating the wisdom to all. Did the Kabbalah always use the Hebrew or Aramaic alphabet or were other alphabets used for similar purposes before the Bronze Age collapse? Thanks for your time and all you do.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
The Cabala wasn't originally Jewish at all. Gershom Scholem documented this years ago in his magisterial book The Origins of the Kabbalah, and more recent research has taken it further back. The core concepts of the Cabala originally emerged in Greek and Latin Middle Platonism and early Neopythagoreanism -- you can find an extraordinary amount of it in the essays of Plutarch, for example -- and passed from there into the Gnostic tradition. The Tree of Life appears in the writings of Celsus nearly a millennium before its first trace in Jewish literature.

The Jewish Kabbalah came into being in the late 11th century in southern France, where it drew extensively on the same Gnostic revival that led to the rise of the Albigensians and shaped the Grail legend so powerfully. At least a century before then, the same concepts had already reached China, where they gave rise to a recognizable Taoist equivalent of the Tree of Life:
[IMAGE:0]
That is to say, yes, I've heard this same drivel about cultural appropriation before, and -- unlike the people who are busy being professionally offended -- I took the time to do a little historical research. I also note that these people are perfectly happy to wear pajamas and use shampoo, even though both of those were culturally appropriated from the Hindus! (Those very words are mildly garbled Hindi.) I don't recommend arguing with the people just mentioned, though; your best bet is to walk away, and keep practicing magic.

replies.title: Re: Geomancy
replies.content: One of the things that annoys me about most modern presentations of geomancy is that they give those traditional rules without telling you what they mean and why they matter. If you get Rubeus in the first house, that means that the querent is lying to you -- if you've cast the reading for your own question, it means you're lying to yourself. Worshiping Santa Claus would be right up there with golden calves! That poor hijacked saint certainly shouldn't have been made into an object for idolatry, though, like all saints, he does make for a wonderful object for contemplation and reflection.

Divorced from his cherubic, Coca-Cola-swilling distortions, Saint Nicholas was a generous giver of gifts. That trait then inspired the faithful to give gifts on his feast day, December 6th in the West and December 19th in the East. Since that kind of venerating of saints was so anathema to the Reformationists, they encouraged gifts to be given on the day of Christ's Mass instead. Who knew that the desire to increase one brand's market share over its competitors so seriously predated industrialism's arrival? We Protestants have been fanatically evangelizing our brand, including all its questionable Christmas trappings, ever since.

As for the fat man's coat, sled, and steeds, those all point towards Shamanic sources. Fur-lined coats and hats are essential winter survival gear on the taiga, and sleds were the only functional vehicles through the snowy season. If there is some mystical tradition other than Shamanism that involves riding up smoke columns escaping from chimney holes cut in roofs to fly around with reindeer spirit guides in astral realms, I have yet to hear of it. Whatever songs may have been joiked to entice reindeer spirits into those shamans' bodies, I seriously doubt that Rudolph the Red-Nosed Reindeer was ever a part of that canon. Who knows though, maybe today's Sámi youths get a kick out of turning Rudolph into a joik to find out what nosy spirits they might be able to draw down?

— Christophe

comments.title: Foul or fowl?
comments.content:
Merry Christmas JMG!

I wanted to ask about your thoughts on eating meat or fish in regards to your practices.

The reason I ask is because I find throughout the year I waver between the notion that it is not something to worry about and other times I think in terms of the accumulated generations of traumatic experiences animals face in commercial production.

For reference when I am lenient I tend to stick to seafood as they are animals which produce the maximum amount of progeny or meat in conditions I can see.

I do think about this quite a bit and have read of stark differences between cultures and practices and wanted to know how you approach this relationship with nourishment.

Or if you you could recommend a practice around the food that we eat. For example this year I have made more effort to really think on the journey the food makes to get to me and limit distractions during meals.

Thank you!

replies.title: Re: Foul or fowl?
replies.content:
It's quite possible, and reasonable, to consider the ethics of your diet, but that can very easily become an unhealthy obsession -- as can diet in general. Nor is life as an animal on a farm necessarily any worse than life in the wild. Existence is traumatic; as the old joke goes, we're born wet, naked, and screaming, and then things get worse. Is it really that much more terrible to get adequate food, water, and protection from predators, and then to get slaughtered quickly and humanely, than it is to spend your entire short life in a desperate struggle to stay fed, and end that existence being torn to shreds by the teeth of a predator?

I make a point of getting meat from the least corporate source I can manage -- preferably the kind of smaller farm where the animal had only one bad day in its life. But like many people, I don't process vegetable protein very well, so meat is a modest but regular part of my diet, and I accept the karma and go on.

replies.content:

A problem I have in general for a grey area where I am not sure what to do.

My wife was a staunch vegetarian for many years and now eats fish so that has been a large influence, just in learning to cook in a way that fit her preference.

comments.content:
Good morning and merry christmas to all!

Few questions for JMG and the commentariat today.

1) Someone posted the AMORC video on karma last week. It was interesting but it suggested that karma was 2 things:
a) what you fear
b) what you expect.

It also seemed to not take the past life angle as seriously. This seems a big new-agey and psychological, whereas my understanding of older sources is that that karma is
a) deeply conditioned in the past, much or most of it in previous lives;
b) there's zero to very little free will to avoid it. Someone sources are completely determinist, and even Steiner suggests the window of choice to steer karma is very small.

As a part of my kundalini awakening which happened after the last 6 years of brutal negative events including near death, I've been having a lot of realisations and insights into the past and present.

One thing I realised was with many bad events, there was a subconscious pattern from childhood (primarily from my mother's own pattern) of being treated badly that was repeating across my lifetime. It was as if I expected to be hurt, and I inculcated relationships with people who would naturally hurt me or betray me easily. It felt like there was some sort of psycho-emotional imprint on my body/face field, affecting my intentionality, that would draw me to these people and enable these experiences.

I want to know a few things:
1) Would karma work through such a mechanism of psycho-emotional patterns or desires being imprinted on the energy or astral body? That good and bad karma is both imprinted on the energy/astral body and reinforces that consciousness and behavior?

That there's a subconscious narrative being projected by a person that other people's subconscious minds respond to?

2) Would thus fated events have similar, but deeper, imprints waiting to be activated by someone/some event/time?

3) Does this imply that there's atleast two types of karma: a) psycho-emotional patterns of this life that affect our intentionality/willing and cause repeated events to occur until we take charge of our emotions and thoughts, b) deeper psycho-emotional imprints of previous lives that are much harder to dispose off simply by trying to change our intentionality/will, and have to be disposed off through actual experience which causes the energetic/astral imprint to finally evaporate?

4) Does this imply that things are lodged at different levels on the astral and energy body and things like reiki or healing hands can resolve the lower level stuff easily by releasing them from the energy/astral system?

Extended reiki + the kundalini rising for example has fixed a lot of my emotional patterns, including unprocessed anger, excessive lust etc, and now those old desires are hard to repeat even consciously, they have no stimulative effect on my mind and body.

5) Do you agree with the AMORC system of what you fear and what you expect, or do you consider it a simplification for modern adoption?

6) All of this implies changing your life by changing the patterns of consciousness that drive your perception and action towards certain psycho-emotional stimulation towards other healthier stimulation (say love, wisdom, truth, joy), which projects out a different reality/representation etc

replies.title: Re: ..
replies.content:
1) That's certainly one way that such things happen. Karma is complex, and so is the universe in which we live.

2) The imprints are there, but how they're activated depends on how you're dealing with related karmic patterns. You needn't go through exactly the same experience, if you can learn the lessons it's meant to teach in some less traumatic way.

3) No. Karma is karma, and awareness is the universal solvent; no matter what kind of karmic issue you have, you can deal with it in ways that don't involve reliving it.

4) It's not so clear-cut. Yes, some patterns can be dealt with more easily than others, and it also happens tolerably often that you get to deal with some karma in one incarnation but other karma's held over to future lives.

5) I'm sorry to hear that they've watered things down like that.

6) Yes, and that's one of the basic challenges of existence.

comments.title: Psychic House Cleaning
comments.content:
Blessed Alban Arthuan.
Last week, I mentioned that after an exceptionally long stay, my long-overdue houseguest was finally forced out. For Yuletide, my friends gathered and did a ritual which was based on a traditional Latvian celebration wherein they go from house to house and village to village making noise and casting out bad spirits. We modified this as a way of clearing the psychic miasma that my former friend left behind. (After her parting, we are definitely not friends, a fact for which I am more grateful than sad.) I tried to cleanse the space myself, but the negative energy was more than I could deal with, and so was glad to have several friends to offer their help. Now my house feels properly clean and my daily Sphere of Protection can effectively extend beyond me, and include the entire property.
I noticed that, as long as my guest was stuck on my couch generating her negative energies, my attempts to create a SoP around my whole property just didn't work. I thought for a while that by including her in my SoP she would be cleansed, too, but that very obviously did not have any effect. Of course because trying to create a barrier against negative energies cannot include a major source of negative energy. For most of the past couple of years, I've only been casting it around myself, and not my whole place, that was effective enough.
Have you ever had to deal with some psychic aftermath or cleansing that you couldn't handle by yourself? I was just wondering if it is just me that is too weak, or if it is something most mages end up facing at some point? Some psychic goo that needs teamwork to cleanse?

Bruce
AKA Renaissance Man

replies.title: Re: Psychic House Cleaning
replies.content: I haven't had that experience, but then I jumped into magic on the deep end of the pool with Golden Dawn ceremonial magic and I've never encountered anything that could stand up to an hour-long GD ritual of binding and banishing. In your case, there's also the point that you permitted the miasma to become established so your will wasn't really single -- someone else with a whole will would have been able to have an effect you possibly couldn't.

comments.title: Birds and bees and other beings
comments.content:
Not so much looking for an answer as an observation and request for thoughts...

On Monday 18th December a blue tit stunned itself against a window and my partner gathered it up, still alive, and held it in her palms (to prevent cold shock) at the front door and it recovered and flew into a close large Salvia, looked at us, took a dump and then flew off.

On Wednesday 20th December, driving to work through countryside we saw a bird in front of us on the road, got closer it did not move, slowed down, still didn't move, got closer and recognised it as a sparrow hawk. Came to a complete stop just in front of it, then after about 3 secs it flew away from the car and landed on a wall and perched looking at us... we drove on. Never seen a sparrow hawk do anything like that before - it was a week of increased solar activity.

Today, Monday 25th December another blue tit did the same thing. Around 10am, one hour later than first time (9am) - same window, similar process - gather bird and hold in palms until it was ready to fly off - landed in same shrub, had a dump, looked at us for a bit then flew off.
Second blue tit was funny because it was on the ground wings out, but as gathered it scratched its head then returned to being stunned and limp.

Tonight, Monday December 25th at 18:30, a honey bee appeared in the kitchen - granted it is mild, but we've had mild Christmases before and never seen a honey bee at this time of year indoors. No reason for a bee not to be about, but unusual.

We talked about it a lot this evening; almost seems like the universe is trying to attract our attention for some reason... almost like putting things in front of us to see what we'll choose to do - fair enough life seems like that, but this last week has us aflutter with speculation.

Has anyone else been experiencing unexpected encounters?

We had the toad rescue and owl business a year or so ago and that seemed like 'something'... the world seems to be getting stranger and stranger!

replies.content: That's fascinating. I haven't had any similar experiences recently -- anyone else?

comments.content:
Hi JMG!

A few years ago when I first began practicing meditation and learning magic, I was interested in finding my magical name. For about a week, I sat in meditation for an hour each day just repeating the question in my mind, what is my magical name?

Imagery appeared quite frequently, but nothing apparent stood out to me. It was on day 8 of this, that while I was repeating the question, the imagery slowly faded away into nothingness and it was just the question and nothingness in my mind. Then, very blatantly, I heard the name Abraxas which startled me enough to break my meditation.

At this time, I knew nothing of the gnostic deity and don’t recognize the name, though I wouldn’t be surprised if I had read it somewhere and didn’t notice.

What do you make of an experience like this? I’m not so sure that it was a magical name that came to me, but I’m also not sure if it was actually Abraxas presenting itself. The name didn’t seem to resonate within my body, as synchronicities and signs usually do. And I’ve had no further experiences with Abraxas from what I can tell since then.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: I'd be very careful with that. I know it's been fashionable for some time to take the name of a deity as one's own magical name, but that's always seemed arrogant and presumptuous to me. That's one of the reasons I prefer the occultist habit of taking a non-divine name or magical motto, or just skipping the whole business and using your real name.

replies.title: Gods’ names
replies.content:
I completely agree with our esteemed host here! One way to approach this if one feels called to a God-name, is to incorporate it into a compound name. In my faith of Heathenry this is easy to do by adding ‘ vin’ or ‘win or wine’ to the end of the God’s name, indicating that you are a friend of this God. Like Thorsvin or Ingwine. I’m sure other languages have their equivalents. But this does imply a commitment that one must take seriously. I have to agree with JMG that it’s often better to take a name that does not imply the kind of heavy, long term commitment that taking a God name does, even if it is modified as I suggest. It’s better to wait until one has spent years being a God-friend and understands what is involved, before taking such a name if one feels called to it.

As a note of interest, my own given name, Winifred, means ‘friend of peace / frith,’ and has shaped my life accordingly.

Winifred Hodge Rose

replies.title: Re: Gods’ names
replies.content: I envy you that name!  I'd have been happier with names that weren't so relentlessly Judeo-Christian, but that's not the hand I was dealt; I considered taking a new name but decided not to bother. The magical mottoes I've taken as an initiate in several occult orders are of course private; when I became the Grand Archdruid of AODA, I was told that I had to take a Druid name, and settled on Creyr Glas Cynwyddom, "Blue Heron Loremaster" in Welsh, partly for symbolic reasons and partly because I knew that 99.9% of people wouldn't even try to pronounce it, and would keep on calling me JMG instead!

comments.title: Grounding absorbed energy
comments.content:
It happened that I had to calm and solace somebody who was very rattled and scared beyond the ordinary level. It worked well, as far as I can tell, but it had the interesting side effect that afterwards, I was a little bit shaken myself. Judging from the experience and my still rather dim sense for non-material energies I have developed so far, I'd say that I have absorbed a lot of energy from the other person while taking care for him. From here, a variety of questions and options unfold:

1) What to do with the excess energy?
a) Can it be put to use? If yes, is it advisable?
b) If not, how to ground it?
c) Will the other person be effected by what I do with the energy once I am it's "owner"?

2) There are situations, especially when the other person is my child, when I would consider it my duty to play the absorber if it need be. But there are other situations where I might not want to play that part.
a) I guess if you are willing to help in such a situation without having the energy pass through your body you have to somehow help the other one to ground it by himself?
b) Is it thinkable that a person who is working professionally with shaken and scared people could put this energy to use, if properly trained? Or would that be some kind of vampirism? Or has it to be grounded anyway for some reason? At least in my case I didn't ask for the energy. It was there, it had to go somewhere and so I offered myself.

Thank you for the bear and your blessings! Christmas is a very mixed bag for me. The astral weather seems to be very noxious during that time and I find it hard to keep it at bay. The calm of the last days of December("Zwischen den Jahren"), however, works usually better for me. That being said, I wish you a happy time and good success for your various undertakings. Oh and my observation that the CosDoc "is a serialization" (on your blog) wasn't meant as a critique, quite the contrary.

Cheers,
Nachtgurke

replies.title: Re: Grounding absorbed energy
replies.content:
1) It can be put to use if you know how -- and that's not a quick thing to learn. If not, ground it out. That's done by standing or sitting with the feet flat on the floor or ground, and visualizing/feeling the energy flowing down out of you into the earth, where it dissipates. No, the energy doesn't affect the other person -- once it's in you, it's yours.

2) I don't recommend trying to teach a scared and shaken person how to ground energy if they've never done it before! If you can teach them in advance, that's another matter. Otherwise, taking the energy and grounding it is a helpful act.

comments.title: Magical attack
comments.content:
Hi, JMG
I have had a strange experience recently. My stepfathers former partner with whom he had a child recently accused him of very serious crimes. All evidence seems to indicate these allegations are false. My mother contacted a trusted psychic who confirmed our belief in his innocence. The psychic also claimed that this person was using very nefarious magical means to try and attack my mother and my family and also dominate my step father. 2) Yes. The next time you pay a visit you might want to wear one of the red bag amulets described in the Magic Monday FAQ, which is linked in the post; those are very good at chasing off that sort of astral miasma.

comments.title: GSF Initiation
comments.content:
JMG,

Thank you for the blessings of the season and my I offer mine to you and yours as well.

I wanted to report that I conducted my Golden Section Fellowship Initiation on the winter solstice. It was an extremely positive, intensely memorable experience to be sure. If I may report on a few things regarding the experience, I feel it could be helpful to others with the same novice experience I have with the occult in general and such ceremonies. If only to give a data point and not as an expectation for anyone who conducts the initiation ceremony. "Intensity" might be the key operative term for this ceremony.

Perhaps I am oversharing, and if this is the case, please do not post this. I simply want to add my voice again to the TSW mantra.

First, I was as nervous with excitement and anticipation just as if this was happening with a full contingent of people and in a physical outdoor lodge. I imagine this was the case because in the grand scheme of things it really was conducted in this way.

I had obviously read many times through and meditated on the aspects of the initiation ritual before I actually performed it. I had imagined the outdoor lodge described in the Composition of Place. I had imagined the wise teacher in as much detail as possible. I had imagined all of it, thought about it, settled on it.

Then, in the actual ceremony, while similar to the descriptions given, it was not the same as I had imagined it before. Similar yes, but it was far more real, tangible, visceral...I was there, really there. Aspects I had not considered prior were added as detail like the type of breeze, leaves rustling, birds singing, and most significant to me was the light the sun cast around the lodge. It matched the time of year and cast proper shadows. It may seem as if this is insignificant, but for me, it was not. This let me know that I was not the sole creator of this place, but others, whoever or whatever they may be were also participating.

The wise teacher I had imagined did not appear, but someone different who was also wise, kind, full of understanding and patience, happy to be there, and just as honored as I was to be a part of this ceremony. He was his own person, exhibiting a personality outside of the one I had previously imagined. It was very humbling.

Secondly, I had not expected my patron deities to attend and yet there they were, at the edge of the treeline overseeing my initiation. Of course I had prayed to them before I started, but to have them actually be there was also very humbling. I have not experienced that before and am grateful for the sincere interest in my progress.

Even as I type this I can hardly contain my excitement regarding the experience. I truly had not expected this level of intensity. I have taken this very seriously and it’s part of the reason it has taken so long for me to fully commit. I wanted to be sure, and now I have no doubt that this is what I need to be doing right here, right now. The divination result just reemphasized and verified that feeling. I have not had three upright cards in two months, and these were very positive and encouraging.

Did I do everything perfectly? No. Did I make a few mistakes? Yes. Did I nervously laugh at those mistakes in the middle of the ceremony? Yes, and so did those in attendance in a loving and understanding way. We moved on and it was one of the most emotional and intense experiences in my life. What I can say is that at least for me, this was a meaningful, humbling, successful initiation ceremony.

Do I have much more to learn? Oh heavens yes! I am well aware that I have barely stepped foot through the gate at the beginning of this path, but I have finally stepped through, and that is what matters most right now.

JMG, I’m sure I cannot thank you enough for all of the hard work and effort it took over the years to put this together in it’s current format. A huge Thank You! anyway. What I have to offer in return right now is to continue the path and see where it leads. As other ways to show my thanks become apparent I will oblige.

Warm Regards,
Bert

comments.content:
Dear JMG,

Continuing previous discussions on Jung and archetypes:

1. Are archetypes basically patterns of consciousness that organise phenomena (give rise to order, boundaries, attention, perception) that drive subconscious and conscious human action?
2. Are the equivalent to forms in platonism or idealism except Jung considers them purely subjective whereas forms are some independent basis (on the astral or mental plane)?
3. Would Jung’s archetypes be a gradation of the will in Schopenhauer conception, or the hinge propositions in a language game in Wittgenstein’s conception?
4. The lowest level ones such as god or other core archetypes (mother? Father?) are almost the core organizational principle via which the mind projects representations that it experiences, including narratives of a higher level (such as narrative of the social contact) which stack on top of lower level archetypes?
5. Do actual gods/higher spirits basically operate via energy sent via these symbolic representations in the astral and mental plane (which is all we can experience)?
6. Is Jung basically doing a cop-out by calling these subjective mental structures but basically allowing all the properties of external spirits/gods to be exercised via them?
7. Why is there danger in going too deep to explore them (such as the health issues Jung faced or Nietzsche’s issues)?
8. What books would you recommend to get a deeper understanding of these concepts? I have read a couple of Jung’s books but I’m trying to get a deeper handle on this

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Er, each of those would take a good-sized essay to answer, the whole set would require a small book, and in most of them you've already presupposed an answer which by and large isn't mine. I think one of those questions is a more reasonable request for a Magic Monday, don't you? Name one -- before 11 pm eastern time, please -- and I'll respond.

replies.title: q
replies.content:
That's fair. Could you answer number 7?

7. Why is there danger in going too deep to explore them (such as the health issues Jung faced or Nietzsche’s issues)?

replies.title: Re: q
replies.content: Nietzche's issues, as far as I know, didn't have anything to do with archetypes -- tertiary syphilis is a much more likely cause, though I won't rule out a plain old ordinary psychotic break. As for the archetypes themselves, Jung's hypothesis is that they're the subjective expression of the basic instincts that we share with animals, and thus they're hardwired into our biology. Mess with them and you're potentially messing with things at the autonomic level of the nervous system, which can certainly screw up your health!

If I missed anybody, or if you would like to add a prayer request for yourself or anyone who has given you consent (or for whom a relevant person holds power of consent) to the list, please feel free to leave a comment below.
 

***LAST CALL***

At the end of this year I will pruning all prayer requests which were made before July 1st, 2023 from the list entirely, with the exception of any that my own intuition tells me ought to be kept on the list. I make no claims to the infallibility of my intuition, so if your entry is older than that, and you would definitely like it to remain on the prayer list, please send a note updating your request.
 

* * *

This week I would like to bring special attention to the following prayer requests.

May Quin's two year old daughter have sustained no permanent brain injury during her recent convulsive episode, and may she be blessed, protected, and continue to develop in the way that benefits her most, iin the manner most in keeping with her nature and those of the deity you pray to.

Tyler A's pregnant wife Monika is at high rish for an ectopic pregnancy. May it turn out that the fetus has implanted in the right place, and may mother and child enjoy good health going forward.

May Frank Rudolf Hartman of Altadena California (picture), who is receiving chemotherapy, be completely cured of the lymphoma that is afflicting him, and may he return to full health. 

May the mass which upon which Yuccaglauca's mother Monica is having a biopsy performed turn out to be entirely benign and safe; may she experience healing and improvement in her situation and overall health.

May the brain surgery that Erika's partner James underwent for his cancer on October 16th have gone successfully; and may he be blessed, healed and protected, and successfully treated for all of his cancer.

May Kyle's friend Amanda, who though in her early thirties is undergoing various difficult treatments for brain cancer, make a full recovery; and may her body and spirit heal with grace.

Lp9's hometown, East Palestine, Ohio, for the safety and welfare of their people, animals and all living beings in and around East Palestine, and to improve the natural environment there to the benefit of all.

comments.title: Christmas tree
comments.content: Happy Monday JMG, this holiday season was the first time I had a live tree. Was never really a fan, especially when I see them discarded later but its presence has filled me with a joy I didn’t expect. I was told they love ice water and every time I can, I drop in two overflowing cups of ice into its basin and find myself in reverence of our new houseguest. I’ve tried to get myself into the spirit of the holiday with music but it’s been a subdued season and the closest I get to prayer is by staring at our little tree. Maybe I’m having more of a Druid experience here than what I consider Christmas? Or maybe this is what live tree proponents have known all along?

replies.title: Re: Christmas tree
replies.content: Having a nurturing relationship with something alive and growing is a very powerful thing. I hope you're planning on planting it somewhere suitable once the holiday season is over.
